lines: Patient is a 65-year-old male with a history of hypertension and diabetes.
He presents with a 2-day history of fever, chills, and cough.
Vital signs: temperature 38.5 C, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm.
Physical exam reveals lung crackles and wheezing.
Lab results: lactate 2.5 mmol/L, creatinine 1.2 mg/dL, platelets 80 10^9/L.
Patient is suspected to have sepsis and will be started on broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Will monitor vital signs and lab results closely.
Patient's MAP is 60 mmHg, which is concerning for hypotension.
Will consider starting vasopressors if blood pressure does not improve.
Nursing: patient tolerating oral intake as ordered.
Nursing: skin warm and dry; no new rashes documented.
Nursing: pain score reviewed; analgesia as needed.
Provider: reviewed overnight events with bedside nurse.
Provider: active problems list updated in the chart.
Provider: medication reconciliation performed.
Patient's condition has worsened overnight.
Temperature is now 39.0 C, blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg, and heart rate is 120 bpm.
Lactate level has increased to 3.0 mmol/L, creatinine has risen to 1.5 mg/dL, and platelets have decreased to 60 10^9/L.
Patient is requiring increasing amounts of oxygen and is being considered for intubation.
Vasopressors have been started to support blood pressure.
Patient's altered mentation is a concern, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 12.
Will continue to monitor patient's condition closely and adjust treatment as needed.
Patient's labs and vitals will be checked regularly to assess for any signs of improvement or deterioration.
Nursing: patient tolerating oral intake as ordered.
Nursing: skin warm and dry; no new rashes documented.
Nursing: pain score reviewed; analgesia as needed.
Provider: reviewed overnight events with bedside nurse.
Provider: active problems list updated in the chart.
Provider: medication reconciliation performed.
Provider: code status confirmed with patient or surrogate.
Patient was admitted with suspected sepsis and was started on broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Patient's condition improved with treatment, with lactate levels decreasing to 1.5 mmol/L and creatinine decreasing to 1.0 mg/dL.
Patient's platelet count increased to 100 10^9/L, and blood pressure improved to 100/60 mmHg.
Patient was weaned off vasopressors and was able to be extubated.
Patient's altered mentation improved, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 15.
Patient was discharged home with follow-up appointments with primary care and infectious disease specialists.
Nursing: patient tolerating oral intake as ordered.
Nursing: skin warm and dry; no new rashes documented.
Nursing: pain score reviewed; analgesia as needed.
Provider: reviewed overnight events with bedside nurse.
Provider: active problems list updated in the chart.
Provider: medication reconciliation performed.
Provider: code status confirmed with patient or surrogate.
Labs: pending studies reviewed when available.
Plan: continue current monitoring and supportive care. Synthetic volume-test variant sepsis_039; no real patient data.
